does: This paper introduces Vocabulary-Aligned Sparse Autoencoder (VASAE), which trains sparse autoencoders with a vocabulary-aligned anchoring constraint to automatically assign interpretable token names to learned features. The method constrains SAE features during training so that each feature's nearest token embedding provides an intrinsic name, avoiding post-hoc naming. The authors demonstrate that VASAE maintains reconstruction quality comparable to standard SAEs while achieving strong alignment: approximately 90% of features in shallow layers of GPT-2-small align to their nearest tokens with a 0.8 cutoff threshold, and 92.8% alignment in shallow layers of Llama-3.1-8B. Case studies show that after subtracting sentence-level mean sparse codes, the intrinsic token names remain semantically relevant to surrounding model computations.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of Transformer models, extending prior methods that decompose residual streams into interpretable features. It responds to a fundamental limitation in existing SAE research: features are typically named retrospectively through manual inspection or auxiliary analysis rather than being inherently connected to the model's token vocabulary. The vocabulary-aligned anchoring approach combines ideas from representation learning with the sparse coding framework, drawing on the intuition that meaningful features should correspond to meaningful units in the model's learned vocabulary space. This extends recent work on feature attribution and interpretability that seeks to ground learned representations in linguistically meaningful quantities.
enables: This method enables more transparent and automatically interpretable sparse autoencoders that could accelerate mechanistic interpretability research by reducing reliance on manual feature naming and post-hoc analysis. The vocabulary-aligned framework suggests future work on understanding how token embeddings relate to learned latent features across model depths, particularly explaining why deeper layers show weaker alignment. It opens directions for using named dictionaries to trace information flow through transformers more directly, to validate whether sparse codes genuinely capture semantically meaningful computations, and potentially to improve model editing or control by manipulating vocabulary-grounded features. The approach could also inform better SAE training objectives that jointly optimize for both sparsity and interpretability from the ground up.
